---
title: "Trophic Index Analysis Script"
author: "Cyrill Hofer"
date: "`r format(Sys.Date(), '%Y-%m-%d')`"
output:
  html_document:
    toc: true
    toc_depth: 2
    df_print: paged
  pdf_document:
    toc: true
    toc_depth: 2
---

```{r}
# =============================================================================
# BLOCK 0 — Setup and data loading
# =============================================================================

rm(list = ls())
gc()
options(stringsAsFactors = FALSE)

suppressPackageStartupMessages({
  library(dplyr)
  library(readr)
  library(ggplot2)
  library(ggrepel)
})

# ---- Working directory ----
setwd("/SynologyDrive/PhD/Projects/SpringBloom2021/DataAnalysis_SpringBloom2021/Figure_03/TrophicIndex/")

# ---- Load data ----
train_dat <- read_csv("IndicatorSpecies_GenomicProperties.csv")
lake_dat  <- read_csv("Species_GenomicProperties_ObservedVsEstimated.csv")

# ---- Core metadata columns ----
meta_cols <- c("Species_ID", "Taxonomy", "Accession", "Classification")

# ---- Enforce trophic class order (training data only) ----
train_dat <- train_dat %>%
  mutate(Classification = factor(
    Classification,
    levels = c("Oligotroph", "Mesotroph", "Copiotroph")
  ))

# ---- Minimal sanity check ----
cat("\nTraining set:", nrow(train_dat), "species\n")
print(table(train_dat$Classification, useNA = "ifany"))

# ---- Consistent colors for all plots ----
trophic_cols <- c(
  Oligotroph = "#2166AC",
  Mesotroph  = "grey60",
  Copiotroph = "#B2182B"
)

# ---- seed for reproducibility of bootstraps ---- 
set.seed(1234)

glimpse(train_dat)
glimpse(lake_dat)
```

# how correlated are the variables ?
```{r}
# =============================================================================
# BLOCK 1 — Feature table + correlation structure (pre-PCA QC; ALL traits)
# =============================================================================

# ---- 1.1 Metadata + candidate genomic traits (training data only) ----
meta_cols <- c("Species_ID", "Classification")

dat_feat <- train_dat %>%
  select(
    all_of(meta_cols),
    GC,
    GENOME_LENGTH,
    TOTAL_TRNA,
    MAX_RRNA,
    CODING_DENSITY,
    MEAN_INTERGENIC_SPACER,
    NUM_SIGMA,
    NUM_SIGNALTRANS
  )

# ---- 1.2 Correlation matrix (Pearson; numeric traits only) ----
feat_mat <- dat_feat %>%
  select(-all_of(meta_cols)) %>%
  as.matrix()

cor_mat <- cor(feat_mat, use = "pairwise.complete.obs", method = "pearson")
cat("\nCorrelation matrix (Pearson r):\n")
print(round(cor_mat, 2))

# ---- 1.3 Rank strongest correlations (|r|) ----
cor_long <- as.data.frame(as.table(cor_mat), stringsAsFactors = FALSE)
names(cor_long) <- c("Trait_1", "Trait_2", "r")

cor_long <- cor_long %>%
  filter(Trait_1 != Trait_2) %>%
  mutate(pair = paste(pmin(Trait_1, Trait_2), pmax(Trait_1, Trait_2), sep = " :: ")) %>%
  group_by(pair) %>%
  summarise(
    Trait_1 = first(pmin(Trait_1, Trait_2)),
    Trait_2 = first(pmax(Trait_1, Trait_2)),
    r = first(r),
    abs_r = abs(first(r)),
    .groups = "drop"
  ) %>%
  arrange(desc(abs_r))

cat("\nTop 15 absolute correlations (|r|):\n")
print(head(cor_long, 15))

# ---- 1.4 Correlation heatmap ----
ggplot(as.data.frame(as.table(cor_mat)), aes(Var1, Var2, fill = Freq)) +
  geom_tile(color = "white", linewidth = 0.2) +
  scale_fill_gradient2(
    low = trophic_cols["Oligotroph"],
    mid = "white",
    high = trophic_cols["Copiotroph"],
    midpoint = 0,
    limits = c(-1, 1),
    name = "Pearson r"
  ) +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 12) +
  theme(
    axis.text.x = element_text(angle = 45, hjust = 1),
    panel.grid = element_blank()
  ) +
  labs(
    title = "Correlation structure of genomic traits (training set)",
    subtitle = "All candidate traits included; rRNA summarized as MAX_RRNA",
    x = NULL,
    y = NULL
  )
```
### Correlation analysis and genomic feature selection

- To construct a genomic trophic index, we first examined pairwise correlations among all candidate genomic traits using the curated training set (n = 22 species).
- This analysis was used to identify redundancy and ensure that retained features captured independent biological dimensions.

#### Genome architecture and streamlining
- Genome length was strongly anticorrelated with coding density (r = −0.85) and strongly positively correlated with mean intergenic spacer length (r = 0.85).
- Coding density and intergenic spacer length were nearly perfectly anticorrelated (r ≈ −1), indicating that they represent inverse measures of the same genome streamlining axis.
- Mean intergenic spacer length was therefore excluded, and coding density was retained as a direct and widely used proxy for genome compactness.

#### Translational capacity
- Total tRNA copy number was strongly correlated with rRNA operon copy number (r = 0.87) and moderately correlated with genome length (r = 0.69), indicating redundancy among translational proxies.
- To avoid collinearity, only a single translational proxy was retained.
- Total tRNA copy number was selected because it provides a continuous, higher-resolution measure and is less sensitive to assembly fragmentation than discrete rRNA operon counts.
- rRNA operon counts were therefore excluded from further analyses.

#### Regulatory capacity
- Regulatory traits (sigma factors and signal transduction genes) were strongly correlated with each other (r = 0.72) and with genome length (r = 0.73–0.78), indicating that regulatory complexity largely scales with genome expansion.
- To prevent over-representation of genome size–driven variation, only one regulatory proxy was retained.
- Sigma factor count was selected as a direct indicator of transcriptional reprogramming capacity.

#### GC content
- GC content showed only weak correlations with genome size and other trophic traits and was effectively orthogonal to the dominant genome streamlining axis.
- Because GC content is strongly influenced by phylogenetic and mutational constraints rather than ecological life-history strategy, it was excluded from trophic index construction.

#### Final feature set
- The final genomic feature set used for PCA and trophic index construction comprised:
  - Genome length (metabolic breadth / genome expansion)
  - Coding density (genome streamlining)
  - Total tRNA copy number (translational investment)
  - Sigma factor count (regulatory flexibility)

- This parsimonious feature set minimizes redundancy, preserves biological interpretability, and is appropriate for multivariate analysis given the size of the training dataset.

```{r}
# =============================================================================
# BLOCK 2 — PCA on curated genomic traits (training set)
#
# Goal:
#   - Reduce correlated genomic traits into a dominant life-history axis
#   - Define PC1 as a candidate genomic trophic index
#
# Reviewer-facing rationale:
#   - Traits were pre-filtered for redundancy (BLOCK 1)
#   - Variables are z-scaled to remove unit effects
#   - PC1 orientation is fixed for biological interpretability
#
# Requirements:
#   - dat_feat from BLOCK 1 (training species only)
#   - trophic_cols from BLOCK 0
# =============================================================================

suppressPackageStartupMessages({
  library(dplyr)
  library(ggplot2)
  library(tibble)
})

# -----------------------------
# 2.1 Final PCA trait set (curated)
# -----------------------------
# Selected based on correlation screening and biological interpretability:
#   - GENOME_LENGTH   : genome expansion / metabolic breadth
#   - CODING_DENSITY  : genome streamlining
#   - TOTAL_TRNA      : translational investment
#   - NUM_SIGMA       : regulatory flexibility

pca_vars <- c(
  "GENOME_LENGTH",
  "CODING_DENSITY",
  "TOTAL_TRNA",
  "NUM_SIGMA"
)

# -----------------------------
# 2.2 Assemble PCA input matrix
# -----------------------------
pca_df <- dat_feat %>%
  select(Species_ID, Classification, all_of(pca_vars))

X <- as.matrix(pca_df[, pca_vars])

# -----------------------------
# 2.3 Standardize traits (z-score)
# -----------------------------
# Critical: removes scale/unit dominance (e.g. bp vs gene counts)
X_scaled <- scale(X, center = TRUE, scale = TRUE)

# -----------------------------
# 2.4 Fit PCA
# -----------------------------
# PCA is run on the already standardized matrix
pca_res <- prcomp(X_scaled, center = FALSE, scale. = FALSE)

# -----------------------------
# 2.5 Variance explained
# -----------------------------
eig_vals <- pca_res$sdev^2
var_exp  <- eig_vals / sum(eig_vals)

pca_var_df <- tibble(
  PC = paste0("PC", seq_along(var_exp)),
  Eigenvalue = eig_vals,
  Variance_Explained = var_exp,
  Cumulative_Variance = cumsum(var_exp)
)

cat("\nPCA variance explained:\n")
print(pca_var_df)

# -----------------------------
# 2.6 PCA scores (species positions)
# -----------------------------
scores <- as.data.frame(pca_res$x) %>%
  mutate(
    Species_ID = pca_df$Species_ID,
    Classification = pca_df$Classification
  )

# -----------------------------
# 2.7 PCA loadings (trait contributions)
# -----------------------------
loadings <- as.data.frame(pca_res$rotation) %>%
  rownames_to_column("Trait")

cat("\nPCA loadings (rounded):\n")
print(loadings %>% mutate(across(where(is.numeric), ~ round(.x, 3))))

# -----------------------------
# 2.8 Orient PC1 for biological interpretability
# -----------------------------
# Sign convention:
#   Positive PC1 = copiotroph-like direction
#   (anchored by larger genomes)

if (loadings$PC1[loadings$Trait == "GENOME_LENGTH"] < 0) {
  message("Flipping PC1 so GENOME_LENGTH loads positively.")

  scores$PC1 <- -scores$PC1
  loadings$PC1 <- -loadings$PC1
  pca_res$x[, "PC1"] <- -pca_res$x[, "PC1"]
  pca_res$rotation[, "PC1"] <- -pca_res$rotation[, "PC1"]
}

# -----------------------------
# 2.9 PCA score plot (PC1 vs PC2)
# -----------------------------
ggplot(scores, aes(PC1, PC2, color = Classification)) +
  geom_point(size = 3, alpha = 0.9) +
  scale_color_manual(values = trophic_cols) +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 13) +
  labs(
    title = "PCA of curated genomic traits (indicator species)",
    subtitle = paste0(
      "PC1 = ", round(var_exp[1] * 100, 1), "% | ",
      "PC2 = ", round(var_exp[2] * 100, 1), "% variance explained"
    ),
    x = "PC1 (genomic trophic index axis)",
    y = "PC2"
  ) +
  theme(
    legend.position = "top",
    panel.grid.minor = element_blank()
  )

```
### PCA of curated genomic traits (training indicator species)

- Principal component analysis of the curated genomic trait set revealed a **strongly dominant first principal component (PC1)**.
- PC1 explained **75.9% of the total variance**, while PC2 explained **12.8%**; all remaining components each explained <10% of the variance.
- This steep variance drop indicates that the genomic trait space of the indicator species is effectively **one-dimensional**.

#### Loadings and biological interpretation of PC1
- PC1 showed coordinated positive and negative loadings across traits that reflect **genome-scale life-history investment**:
  - **GENOME_LENGTH** loaded strongly and positively (after sign orientation), indicating increasing genomic and metabolic breadth.
  - **TOTAL_TRNA** and **NUM_SIGMA** loaded positively, reflecting increasing translational capacity and regulatory flexibility.
  - **CODING_DENSITY** loaded negatively, consistent with reduced genome streamlining at higher PC1 values.
- This loading structure is consistent with a continuum from **streamlined oligotrophs** (small, compact genomes with limited regulatory capacity) to **copiotrophs** (larger genomes with greater translational and regulatory investment).

#### Separation of a priori trophic classes
- Indicator species classified a priori as oligotrophs, mesotrophs, and copiotrophs showed a clear gradient along PC1.
- Oligotrophic taxa clustered at low PC1 values, copiotrophic taxa at high PC1 values, and mesotrophs occupied intermediate positions.
- No sharp clustering or discrete separation was observed along PC2, supporting the interpretation that trophic strategies form a **continuous spectrum rather than discrete categories**.

#### Orientation and robustness
- Because PCA sign is arbitrary, PC1 was oriented such that **GENOME_LENGTH loads positively**, ensuring that increasing PC1 values correspond to increasingly copiotrophic genomic strategies.
- This orientation does not affect relative species ordering and was applied solely to enhance biological interpretability.

#### Conclusion
- The PCA identifies a **single dominant axis** of coordinated genomic variation that aligns with known trophic life-history strategies.
- Together with subsequent robustness and permutation-based significance tests, these results justify the use of **PC1 as a continuous genomic trophic index** for downstream analyses and projection onto lake-wide genomic datasets.


```{r}
# =============================================================================
# BLOCK 3 — Robustness tests for the trophic index (training set)
#
# Purpose:
#   - Demonstrate that the PC1-based trophic axis is not driven by any single trait
#   - Quantify stability of species ordering when removing key features
#
# What this block does:
#   (A) Re-fit PCA after removing GENOME_LENGTH
#   (B) Re-fit PCA after removing CODING_DENSITY
#   (C) (Optional stress test) Re-fit PCA after adding redundant traits
#       (MAX_RRNA and NUM_SIGNALTRANS), then check if PC1 ordering persists
#
# Requirements (already produced in BLOCK 2):
#   - dat_feat from BLOCK 1
#   - scores from BLOCK 2 (must contain Species_ID, Classification, PC1)
#   - pca_vars from BLOCK 2 (curated feature set)
#
# Notes for reviewers:
#   - PCA sign is arbitrary; we align PC1 orientation to the Full model using correlation
#   - Comparisons are performed on the intersecting set of species (inner join by Species_ID)
# =============================================================================

suppressPackageStartupMessages({
  library(dplyr)
  library(tibble)
  library(ggplot2)
})

# -----------------------------
# 3.1 Helper: fit PCA on a given trait set (z-scaled)
# -----------------------------
fit_pca_scaled <- function(dat, vars) {
  X <- dat %>%
    select(all_of(vars)) %>%
    mutate(across(everything(), as.numeric)) %>%
    as.matrix()

  Xs <- scale(X, center = TRUE, scale = TRUE)
  prcomp(Xs, center = FALSE, scale. = FALSE)
}

# -----------------------------
# 3.2 Helper: compute PC1 and align its sign to the Full model (scores$PC1)
# -----------------------------
get_aligned_pc1 <- function(dat_feat, vars, scores_full) {

  pca_tmp <- fit_pca_scaled(dat_feat, vars)

  sc <- as.data.frame(pca_tmp$x) %>%
    mutate(
      Species_ID = dat_feat$Species_ID,
      Classification = dat_feat$Classification
    ) %>%
    select(Species_ID, Classification, PC1, PC2)

  # Align sign using correlation to Full PC1 on overlapping Species_IDs
  joined <- inner_join(
    scores_full %>% select(Species_ID, PC1_full = PC1),
    sc %>% select(Species_ID, PC1_test = PC1),
    by = "Species_ID"
  )

  r <- cor(joined$PC1_full, joined$PC1_test, use = "complete.obs")

  flipped <- FALSE
  if (!is.na(r) && r < 0) {
    sc$PC1 <- -sc$PC1
    flipped <- TRUE
    r <- -r
  }

  list(scores = sc, cor_to_full = r, flipped = flipped, pca = pca_tmp)
}

# -----------------------------
# 3.3 Define robustness models
# -----------------------------
# Full curated model (from BLOCK 2)
pca_vars_full <- pca_vars

# Model A: remove GENOME_LENGTH
pca_vars_no_size <- setdiff(pca_vars_full, "GENOME_LENGTH")

# Model B: remove CODING_DENSITY
pca_vars_no_density <- setdiff(pca_vars_full, "CODING_DENSITY")

# Optional stress model: add redundant traits (NOT used for the index)
pca_vars_stress <- c("GENOME_LENGTH", "CODING_DENSITY", "TOTAL_TRNA", "NUM_SIGMA",
                     "MAX_RRNA", "NUM_SIGNALTRANS")

# -----------------------------
# 3.4 Run robustness PCA fits (PC1 aligned to Full model)
# -----------------------------
res_no_size    <- get_aligned_pc1(dat_feat, pca_vars_no_size, scores)
res_no_density <- get_aligned_pc1(dat_feat, pca_vars_no_density, scores)

cat("\nRobustness: correlation to Full PC1 (sign-aligned)\n")
cat(" - No GENOME_LENGTH:  r =", round(res_no_size$cor_to_full, 3),
    " | flipped =", res_no_size$flipped, "\n")
cat(" - No CODING_DENSITY: r =", round(res_no_density$cor_to_full, 3),
    " | flipped =", res_no_density$flipped, "\n")

# Variance explained (PC1) for each model
get_pc1_var <- function(pca_obj) (pca_obj$sdev[1]^2) / sum(pca_obj$sdev^2)

pc1_var_full <- var_exp[1]  # from BLOCK 2
pc1_var_no_size <- get_pc1_var(res_no_size$pca)
pc1_var_no_density <- get_pc1_var(res_no_density$pca)

robust_summary <- tibble(
  Model = c("Full (curated)", "No GENOME_LENGTH", "No CODING_DENSITY"),
  PC1_Variance_Explained = c(pc1_var_full, pc1_var_no_size, pc1_var_no_density),
  PC1_Correlation_to_Full = c(1, res_no_size$cor_to_full, res_no_density$cor_to_full)
) %>%
  mutate(across(where(is.numeric), ~ round(.x, 4)))

cat("\nRobustness summary:\n")
print(robust_summary)

# -----------------------------
# 3.5 Diagnostic plots: PC1 agreement (Full vs each reduced model)
# -----------------------------
pc1_compare_plot <- function(scores_full, scores_test, test_label, r_val) {
  df <- inner_join(
    scores_full %>% select(Species_ID, PC1_full = PC1, Classification),
    scores_test %>% select(Species_ID, PC1_test = PC1),
    by = "Species_ID"
  )

  ggplot(df, aes(PC1_full, PC1_test, color = Classification)) +
    geom_point(size = 3, alpha = 0.9) +
    scale_color_manual(values = trophic_cols) +
    theme_minimal(base_size = 13) +
    theme(legend.position = "top", panel.grid.minor = element_blank()) +
    labs(
      title = paste0("PC1 robustness: Full vs ", test_label),
      subtitle = paste0("Pearson r = ", round(r_val, 3), " (sign-aligned)"),
      x = "PC1 (Full curated model)",
      y = paste0("PC1 (", test_label, ")")
    )
}

pc1_compare_plot(scores, res_no_size$scores, "No GENOME_LENGTH", res_no_size$cor_to_full)
pc1_compare_plot(scores, res_no_density$scores, "No CODING_DENSITY", res_no_density$cor_to_full)

# -----------------------------
# 3.6 Optional: stress test with redundant traits
# -----------------------------
# This tests whether the axis is stable even under deliberate redundancy.
if (all(pca_vars_stress %in% colnames(dat_feat))) {

  res_stress <- get_aligned_pc1(dat_feat, pca_vars_stress, scores)

  pc1_var_stress <- get_pc1_var(res_stress$pca)

  cat("\nStress test (redundant traits):\n")
  cat(" - PC1 variance explained:", round(pc1_var_stress, 4), "\n")
  cat(" - Correlation to Full PC1 (sign-aligned):", round(res_stress$cor_to_full, 3),
      " | flipped =", res_stress$flipped, "\n")

  # Plot agreement
  pc1_compare_plot(scores, res_stress$scores, "Stress (redundant traits)", res_stress$cor_to_full)

} else {
  message("\nStress test skipped: MAX_RRNA and/or NUM_SIGNALTRANS not found in dat_feat.")
}
```

### Robustness analysis of the genomic trophic index

- To assess whether the genomic trophic index (PC1) is driven by any single trait or by redundancy among correlated features, we performed a series of robustness analyses based on feature removal and redundancy stress tests.
- In all cases, PCA was re-fit on standardized traits, and PC1 orientation was aligned to the full curated model to allow direct comparison of species ordering.

#### Robustness to genome size removal
- Removing genome length from the PCA reduced the variance explained by PC1 only modestly (from 75.9% to 71.5%).
- PC1 scores from the reduced model were almost perfectly correlated with those from the full model (Pearson r = 0.992), differing only by axis orientation.
- Species ordering along PC1 was preserved, indicating that the trophic index is not a trivial proxy for genome size alone.

#### Robustness to genome architecture (coding density) removal
- Excluding coding density from the PCA did not reduce the dominance of PC1; variance explained by PC1 remained high (77.6%).
- PC1 scores from this reduced model were highly correlated with the full model (Pearson r = 0.983).
- This demonstrates that the trophic axis persists even when genome streamlining metrics are removed, and is not dependent on genome architecture alone.

#### Robustness to redundancy (stress test with correlated traits)
- In a deliberate stress test, highly correlated traits (rRNA operon counts and signal transduction gene counts) were reintroduced alongside the curated feature set.
- Despite the inclusion of redundant variables, PC1 still explained a large fraction of total variance (73.0%).
- PC1 scores from the stress test remained strongly correlated with the curated model (Pearson r = 0.99), and species ordering was unchanged.
- This indicates that the trophic axis is not sensitive to feature redundancy and reflects a stable underlying structure.

#### Overall interpretation
- Across all robustness tests, PC1 remained a dominant, one-dimensional axis with stable species ordering.
- The high concordance of PC1 scores across models demonstrates that the trophic index is not driven by genome size, genome architecture, or any single genomic trait.
- Instead, PC1 captures a coordinated genomic investment strategy integrating translational capacity and regulatory flexibility, consistent with a continuum from oligotrophic to copiotrophic life histories.

#### Conclusion
- These robustness analyses provide strong evidence that the genomic trophic index represents a stable and biologically meaningful life-history axis rather than a methodological artifact of PCA or feature selection.

```{r}
# =============================================================================
# BLOCK 5 — Permutation-based PCA significance test (PCAtest-style)
#
# Purpose:
#   - Test whether the curated PCA captures non-random correlational structure
#   - Statistically justify PC1 as a meaningful biological axis
#
# Null model:
#   - Permute values within each trait column
#     (destroys cross-trait correlation, preserves marginal distributions)
#
# Requirements:
#   - dat_feat from BLOCK 1
#   - pca_vars from BLOCK 2 (curated feature set)
#
# Notes:
#   - Uses finite-permutation corrected p-values
#   - Focus is on PC1; other PCs are reported descriptively
# =============================================================================

set.seed(123)

suppressPackageStartupMessages({
  library(dplyr)
  library(tidyr)
  library(tibble)
  library(ggplot2)
  library(patchwork)
})

# -----------------------------
# 5.1 Observed PCA (curated model)
# -----------------------------
X_obs_df <- dat_feat %>%
  select(all_of(pca_vars)) %>%
  mutate(across(everything(), as.numeric))

# PCA requires complete cases
keep <- complete.cases(X_obs_df)
if (any(!keep)) {
  message(sprintf(
    "[PCAtest] Dropping %d/%d rows with NA in curated traits.",
    sum(!keep), nrow(X_obs_df)
  ))
}

X_obs <- as.matrix(X_obs_df[keep, , drop = FALSE])

# Same scaling + PCA as BLOCK 2
X_obs_scaled <- scale(X_obs, center = TRUE, scale = TRUE)
pca_obs <- prcomp(X_obs_scaled, center = FALSE, scale. = FALSE)

eig_obs <- pca_obs$sdev^2
var_obs <- eig_obs / sum(eig_obs)
n_pc <- length(var_obs)

# -----------------------------
# 5.2 Permutation procedure
# -----------------------------
n_perm <- 5000  # reduce to 1000 for quick testing
perm_var <- matrix(NA_real_, nrow = n_perm, ncol = n_pc)

for (i in seq_len(n_perm)) {

  # Permute each column independently
  X_perm <- apply(X_obs, 2, sample)

  X_perm_scaled <- scale(X_perm, center = TRUE, scale = TRUE)
  pca_perm <- prcomp(X_perm_scaled, center = FALSE, scale. = FALSE)

  eig_perm <- pca_perm$sdev^2
  perm_var[i, ] <- eig_perm / sum(eig_perm)
}

colnames(perm_var) <- paste0("PC", seq_len(n_pc))

# -----------------------------
# 5.3 PC-wise permutation p-values (finite-sample corrected)
# -----------------------------
pvals <- sapply(seq_len(n_pc), function(k) {
  (1 + sum(perm_var[, k] >= var_obs[k])) / (1 + n_perm)
})

pval_tbl <- tibble(
  PC = paste0("PC", seq_len(n_pc)),
  Observed_Variance = var_obs,
  P_value = pvals
)

cat("\nPermutation test: variance explained per PC\n")
print(pval_tbl)

# -----------------------------
# 5.4 Null summaries (mean + 95% interval)
# -----------------------------
perm_long <- as.data.frame(perm_var) %>%
  pivot_longer(cols = everything(), names_to = "PC", values_to = "NullVar") %>%
  mutate(PC_num = as.integer(sub("PC", "", PC)))

null_summary <- perm_long %>%
  group_by(PC, PC_num) %>%
  summarise(
    null_mean = mean(NullVar),
    null_lo = quantile(NullVar, 0.025),
    null_hi = quantile(NullVar, 0.975),
    .groups = "drop"
  ) %>%
  arrange(PC_num) %>%
  mutate(obs = var_obs[PC_num])

# -----------------------------
# 5.5 Diagnostic plots
# -----------------------------

# Panel A: PC1 null distribution
pA <- ggplot(as.data.frame(perm_var), aes(x = PC1)) +
  geom_histogram(bins = 30, fill = "grey80", color = "black") +
  geom_vline(xintercept = var_obs[1], color = "red", linewidth = 1) +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 12) +
  labs(
    title = "A) Null distribution for PC1",
    subtitle = paste0(
      "Observed = ", round(var_obs[1], 3),
      " | p ",
      ifelse(pvals[1] < 0.001, "< 0.001", paste0("= ", signif(pvals[1], 3)))
    ),
    x = "Variance explained by PC1 (null)",
    y = "Frequency"
  )

# Panel B: PC2 null distribution
pB <- ggplot(as.data.frame(perm_var), aes(x = PC2)) +
  geom_histogram(bins = 30, fill = "grey80", color = "black") +
  geom_vline(xintercept = var_obs[2], color = "red", linewidth = 1) +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 12) +
  labs(
    title = "B) Null distribution for PC2",
    subtitle = paste0(
      "Observed = ", round(var_obs[2], 3),
      " | p ",
      ifelse(pvals[2] < 0.001, "< 0.001", paste0("= ", signif(pvals[2], 3)))
    ),
    x = "Variance explained by PC2 (null)",
    y = "Frequency"
  )

# Panel C: Observed vs null across PCs
pC <- ggplot(null_summary, aes(x = PC_num)) +
  geom_errorbar(aes(ymin = null_lo, ymax = null_hi), width = 0.15) +
  geom_point(aes(y = null_mean), size = 2) +
  geom_point(aes(y = obs), color = "red", size = 3) +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 12) +
  scale_x_continuous(breaks = seq_len(n_pc)) +
  labs(
    title = "C) Observed vs null variance across PCs",
    subtitle = "Red = observed | Black = null mean ± 95% interval",
    x = "Principal component",
    y = "Variance explained"
  )

# Panel D: Scree plot (observed vs null mean)
pD <- ggplot(null_summary, aes(x = PC_num)) +
  geom_line(aes(y = obs), color = "red", linewidth = 1) +
  geom_point(aes(y = obs), color = "red", size = 2) +
  geom_line(aes(y = null_mean), linetype = "dashed") +
  geom_point(aes(y = null_mean), size = 2) +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 12) +
  scale_x_continuous(breaks = seq_len(n_pc)) +
  labs(
    title = "D) Scree plot: observed vs null",
    subtitle = "Red = observed | Dashed black = null mean",
    x = "Principal component",
    y = "Variance explained"
  )

# -----------------------------
# 5.6 Combine panels
# -----------------------------
(pA | pB) / (pC | pD)
```
### Permutation-based significance testing of the PCA (PCAtest-style)

- To formally test whether the curated PCA captures non-random correlational structure among genomic traits, we applied a permutation-based PCA significance test following the logic of PCAtest.
- The null hypothesis assumes that genomic traits are uncorrelated; this was implemented by permuting values independently within each trait column, thereby preserving marginal distributions while destroying cross-trait covariance.
- PCA was then recomputed for each permuted dataset, and the variance explained by each principal component was compared to the observed values.

#### Significance of PC1
- PC1 explained **75.9%** of the total variance in the observed data.
- Under the null model, the expected variance explained by PC1 was substantially lower, and the observed value lay far outside the null distribution.
- The permutation-based p-value for PC1 was **p = 0.0002**, indicating that PC1 explains significantly more variance than expected under a model of uncorrelated genomic traits.
- This result demonstrates that PC1 reflects a strong, non-random axis of coordinated genomic variation.

#### Higher-order principal components
- PC2 explained **12.8%** of the variance, but this value was entirely consistent with the null distribution (p = 1).
- PC3 and PC4 likewise showed no evidence of explaining more variance than expected under the null model (p = 1 for both).
- These results indicate that higher-order components represent stochastic or residual variation rather than biologically meaningful structure.

#### Overall interpretation
- Permutation-based testing confirms that the PCA identifies a **single statistically significant axis** of genomic variation.
- The absence of significance for PC2 and higher components supports the interpretation of the genomic trophic index as a **one-dimensional life-history axis**, rather than a multidimensional trait space.
- Combined with robustness analyses demonstrating stability to feature removal and redundancy, these results provide strong statistical justification for using PC1 as a continuous genomic trophic index.

#### Conclusion
- The permutation test establishes that PC1 captures genuine, non-random covariance among genomic traits, while higher PCs do not.
- This validates the use of PC1 as the sole axis for downstream ecological interpretation and projection onto lake-wide genomic datasets.

```{r}
# =============================================================================
# BLOCK 6 — Define and compute the Genomic Trophic Index (PC1) + training rank plot
#
# Purpose:
#   - Formalize the trophic index as the PC1 score of a standardized trait matrix
#   - Store the training-set centering/scaling parameters and PC1 loadings
#   - Compute TrophicIndex for each training species
#   - Produce a ranked index plot with taxonomy labels (interpretation only)
#
# This block is intentionally "definition + application" only.
# It assumes you already performed QC / robustness / permutation testing upstream.
#
# Required objects/columns:
#   - dat_feat: training dataframe containing Species_ID, Classification, and traits
#   - train_dat: contains Species_ID and Taxonomy (optional; used for labeling)
#   - trophic_cols: named vector of colors for Classification
#
# Notes:
#   - Index is computed as: TI_i = sum_j w_j * (x_ij - mu_j)/sigma_j
#   - PC1 sign is oriented so GENOME_LENGTH loads positively (copiotroph direction)
#   - If you want the 4-trait curated index, keep the default pca_vars below.
# =============================================================================

suppressPackageStartupMessages({
  library(dplyr)
  library(tibble)
  library(ggplot2)
  library(ggrepel)
})

# -----------------------------
# 6.1 Choose the trait set for the index (DEFAULT = curated 4-trait model)
# -----------------------------
# IMPORTANT:
# Use the same trait set you validated (robustness + permutation testing).
# If your validated model used 4 traits, keep this as-is.
# If you intentionally validated a 6-trait model, replace pca_vars accordingly.

pca_vars <- c(
  "GENOME_LENGTH",
  "CODING_DENSITY",
  "TOTAL_TRNA",
  "NUM_SIGMA"
)

# Sanity check: ensure traits exist
stopifnot(all(pca_vars %in% colnames(dat_feat)))

# -----------------------------
# 6.2 Build standardized training matrix and fit PCA
# -----------------------------
X_train_df <- dat_feat %>%
  select(all_of(pca_vars)) %>%
  mutate(across(everything(), as.numeric))

# PCA requires complete cases
keep <- complete.cases(X_train_df)
if (any(!keep)) {
  message(sprintf("[TrophicIndex] Dropping %d/%d training rows with NA in pca_vars.",
                  sum(!keep), nrow(X_train_df)))
}
X_train_df <- X_train_df[keep, , drop = FALSE]
dat_feat_ti <- dat_feat[keep, , drop = FALSE]

# Training-set centering and scaling parameters (stored for projection later)
train_center <- colMeans(X_train_df)
train_scale  <- apply(X_train_df, 2, sd)

# Standardize using training parameters
X_train_scaled <- sweep(as.matrix(X_train_df), 2, train_center, "-")
X_train_scaled <- sweep(X_train_scaled, 2, train_scale, "/")

# PCA on standardized matrix (do not re-center/scale inside prcomp)
pca_fit <- prcomp(X_train_scaled, center = FALSE, scale. = FALSE)

# Variance explained (optional print; useful in Methods)
eig_vals <- pca_fit$sdev^2
var_exp  <- eig_vals / sum(eig_vals)
cat("\n[TrophicIndex] PCA variance explained:\n")
print(tibble(
  PC = paste0("PC", seq_along(var_exp)),
  Eigenvalue = eig_vals,
  Variance_Explained = var_exp,
  Cumulative_Variance = cumsum(var_exp)
))

# PC1 loadings (weights w_j)
pc1_loadings <- pca_fit$rotation[, "PC1"]
names(pc1_loadings) <- rownames(pca_fit$rotation)

# -----------------------------
# 6.3 Orient PC1 for biological interpretability
# -----------------------------
# Enforce: GENOME_LENGTH has positive loading on PC1
if (pc1_loadings["GENOME_LENGTH"] < 0) {
  message("[TrophicIndex] Flipping PC1 so GENOME_LENGTH loads positively.")
  pc1_loadings <- -pc1_loadings
  pca_fit$x[, "PC1"] <- -pca_fit$x[, "PC1"]
}

# -----------------------------
# 6.4 Compute TrophicIndex for training species (explicit definition)
# -----------------------------
# TrophicIndex_i = (standardized traits) %*% (PC1 loadings)
TrophicIndex <- as.numeric(X_train_scaled %*% pc1_loadings)

dat_feat_ti <- dat_feat_ti %>%
  mutate(
    TrophicIndex = TrophicIndex
  )

# Save the model components needed for later projection onto lake_dat
trophic_index_model <- list(
  pca_vars = pca_vars,
  center = train_center,
  scale = train_scale,
  pc1_loadings = pc1_loadings
)

cat("\n[TrophicIndex] Model definition:\n")
print(trophic_index_model)

# -----------------------------
# 6.5 Ranked training plot (taxonomy labels for interpretation only)
# -----------------------------
# Taxonomy is NOT used to compute the index; it is displayed for interpretability.

plot_df <- dat_feat_ti %>%
  left_join(train_dat %>% select(Species_ID, Taxonomy), by = "Species_ID") %>%
  arrange(TrophicIndex) %>%
  mutate(
    Rank = row_number(),
    Label = ifelse(!is.na(Taxonomy) & Taxonomy != "", Taxonomy, Species_ID)
  )

ggplot(plot_df, aes(Rank, TrophicIndex, color = Classification)) +
  geom_point(size = 3, alpha = 0.9) +
  geom_text_repel(aes(label = Label), size = 3, max.overlaps = Inf) +
  scale_color_manual(values = trophic_cols) +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 12) +
  theme(legend.position = "top") +
  labs(
    title = "Ranked genomic trophic index across indicator species",
    subtitle = "Index = PC1 score of standardized curated genomic traits (taxonomy shown for interpretation only)",
    x = "Species rank (low → high trophic index)",
    y = "TrophicIndex (PC1)"
  )
```
### Critical interpretation of the genomic trophic index (PC1)

- The trophic index is defined as the **PC1 score** of a **standardized genomic trait matrix** (GENOME_LENGTH, CODING_DENSITY, TOTAL_TRNA, NUM_SIGMA), with the sign oriented so that **larger genomes correspond to higher index values**.
- The **PC1 loading structure is internally coherent and biologically interpretable**:
  - **GENOME_LENGTH** loads positively (w = 0.552): larger genomes increase the index.
  - **CODING_DENSITY** loads negatively (w = −0.508): more streamlined genomes decrease the index.
  - **TOTAL_TRNA** loads positively (w = 0.447): higher translational investment increases the index.
  - **NUM_SIGMA** loads positively (w = 0.487): greater regulatory flexibility increases the index.
- Together, these loadings define a single axis contrasting:
  - **streamlined, low-investment genomes** (low index; oligotroph-like)
  - versus **expanded, high-investment genomes** (high index; copiotroph-like).

### Can the index be used despite imperfect alignment with experimental readouts?

- **Yes—provided it is interpreted correctly.** The index quantifies **genomic investment potential**, not realized ecological performance in any single assay.
- Apparent mismatches between index position and experimental readouts are not unexpected because realized phenotypes depend on additional factors not captured by static genomic traits, including:
  - environmental context (nutrient regime, temperature, seasonality),
  - biotic interactions (competition, grazing, viral pressure),
  - physiological state (dormancy, stress history),
  - regulation and expression (traits may be present but not expressed),
  - measurement context (assay conditions may not match ecological niche).
- Therefore, the trophic index should be used primarily as a **continuous ranking** along an oligotroph–copiotroph continuum rather than as a strict categorical classifier.

### Why the index remains statistically defensible

- The trophic axis (PC1) is supported by three independent lines of evidence:
  - **Dominance of PC1**: PC1 explains a large fraction of variance (≈ 0.759), indicating a strong shared genomic strategy axis.
  - **Robustness to feature removal and redundancy**: species ordering along PC1 remains highly stable when removing major contributors (GENOME_LENGTH or CODING_DENSITY) and under redundancy stress tests (r ≈ 0.98–0.99).
  - **Permutation-based PCA diagnostics**: only PC1 explains significantly more variance than expected under a null model of uncorrelated traits (p = 0.0002), whereas PC2–PC4 are fully consistent with the null expectation (p = 1).
- These results demonstrate that PC1 reflects **non-random, coordinated covariance** among genomic traits rather than a single-trait artifact.

### Practical interpretation for downstream analyses

- The trophic index should be described as a **genome-informed life-history strategy axis**, capturing coordinated investment in genome expansion, reduced streamlining, translational capacity, and regulatory flexibility.
- Downstream analyses should treat the index as:
  - a **continuous predictor** (preferred), and/or
  - a basis for **relative ranking** of taxa,
  rather than expecting perfect agreement with every experimental classification.
- Any taxa that contradict expectations should be reported transparently as **exceptions**, discussed as biologically plausible deviations, and not treated as evidence against the validity of the axis itself.


```{r}
# =============================================================================
# BLOCK 7 — Quartile-based operational strategy groups along the TrophicIndex
#
# Purpose:
#   - Define reviewer-safe *operational* categories from a continuous index
#   - Use label-independent, distribution-based thresholds (Q1/Q3)
#   - Visualize the grouping and summarize mapping to a priori labels
#
# Why quartiles are defensible (reviewer-facing, implemented here):
#   - Thresholds are computed from the TrophicIndex distribution (not from labels)
#   - Captures extremes without imposing artificial hard boundaries everywhere
#   - Reproducible rule across datasets; no tuning/hand-picking
#   - Maintains adequate sample sizes for downstream comparisons (esp. n=22)
#
# Inputs expected from prior blocks:
#   - dat_feat_ti : training dataframe produced in BLOCK 6 (contains TrophicIndex)
#   - trophic_cols: colors for Classification
#
# Outputs:
#   - dat_feat_ti updated with ExtremeClass
#   - density plot with quartile cutoffs
#   - (optional) mapping table + stacked bar plot if Classification is present
# =============================================================================

suppressPackageStartupMessages({
  library(dplyr)
  library(tidyr)
  library(ggplot2)
  library(patchwork)
})

# -----------------------------
# 7.1 Quartile thresholds (label-independent)
# -----------------------------
q1 <- unname(quantile(dat_feat_ti$TrophicIndex, 0.25, na.rm = TRUE))
q3 <- unname(quantile(dat_feat_ti$TrophicIndex, 0.75, na.rm = TRUE))

cat("\n[Quartiles] Thresholds for operational groups:\n")
cat(" Q1 (25%) =", round(q1, 4), "\n")
cat(" Q3 (75%) =", round(q3, 4), "\n")

# -----------------------------
# 7.2 Assign operational groups (extremes + intermediate)
# -----------------------------
df_ti <- dat_feat_ti %>%
  mutate(
    ExtremeClass = case_when(
      TrophicIndex <= q1 ~ "Oligo_like",
      TrophicIndex >= q3 ~ "Copio_like",
      TRUE ~ "Intermediate"
    ),
    ExtremeClass = factor(ExtremeClass, levels = c("Oligo_like", "Intermediate", "Copio_like"))
  )

# Stable explicit colors
extreme_cols <- c(
  Oligo_like = "#2166AC",     # match Oligotroph
  Intermediate = "grey60",
  Copio_like = "#B2182B"      # match Copiotroph
)

# -----------------------------
# 7.3 Density plot: operational groups + thresholds
# -----------------------------
p_quartile_density <- ggplot(df_ti, aes(x = TrophicIndex, fill = ExtremeClass, color = ExtremeClass)) +
  geom_density(alpha = 0.35, linewidth = 1) +
  geom_vline(xintercept = c(q1, q3), linetype = "dashed", linewidth = 0.8) +
  scale_fill_manual(values = extreme_cols, drop = FALSE) +
  scale_color_manual(values = extreme_cols, drop = FALSE) +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 13) +
  labs(
    title = "Quartile-based operational strategy groups along the trophic index",
    subtitle = "Extreme quartiles define oligo-like and copio-like groups; middle half remains intermediate",
    x = "TrophicIndex (PC1 score)",
    y = "Density"
  ) +
  theme(legend.position = "top")

# -----------------------------
# 7.4 Mapping to a priori labels (interpretation only; NOT used to define cutoffs)
# -----------------------------
# Only run if Classification exists and is not all NA.
if ("Classification" %in% colnames(df_ti) && !all(is.na(df_ti$Classification))) {

  df_ti$Classification <- factor(df_ti$Classification,
                                levels = c("Oligotroph", "Mesotroph", "Copiotroph"))

  mapping_tbl <- df_ti %>%
    count(Classification, ExtremeClass) %>%
    pivot_wider(names_from = ExtremeClass, values_from = n, values_fill = 0) %>%
    arrange(Classification)

  cat("\n[Quartiles] Mapping of a priori labels onto quartile-defined groups (interpretation only):\n")
  print(mapping_tbl)

  p_mapping <- ggplot(df_ti, aes(x = Classification, fill = ExtremeClass)) +
    geom_bar(position = "fill", color = "white") +
    scale_fill_manual(values = extreme_cols, drop = FALSE) +
    theme_minimal(base_size = 13) +
    labs(
      title = "A priori trophic labels across quartile-defined operational groups",
      subtitle = "Operational groups are index-defined; labels shown only for interpretability",
      x = NULL,
      y = "Proportion within label"
    ) +
    theme(legend.position = "top")

  p_quartile_density | p_mapping

} else {
  p_quartile_density
}

# -----------------------------
# 7.5 Write back into your main object for downstream steps
# -----------------------------
# Keep dat_feat_ti as the canonical training table with TrophicIndex + ExtremeClass.
dat_feat_ti <- df_ti
```
### Reviewer verdict on quartile-based operational grouping

- The quartile-based stratification of the trophic index yields **biologically coherent extremes** while preserving the continuous nature of the underlying axis.
- The *oligo-like* group is strongly enriched for a priori oligotrophic taxa (5/6), with no copiotrophs present.
- The *copio-like* group is enriched for copiotrophic taxa (4/6), with no oligotrophs present; the remaining taxa are mesotrophs, consistent with intermediate strategies.
- The intermediate group contains a mixture of all three a priori classes, as expected under a **continuous trophic strategy spectrum**.

- Importantly, group definitions are **label-independent**, relying solely on index quantiles rather than a priori trophic classifications.
- The observed overlap in the intermediate range is not a weakness but a strength, as it avoids imposing artificial hard boundaries on a continuous genomic trait axis.
- Combined with permutation-based PCA significance testing and robustness analyses, these results support the use of the trophic index as a **statistically grounded, biologically interpretable continuum**, with quartile-based groups serving as **operational extremes** for downstream analyses.

> *We therefore treat the trophic index as the primary variable and use quantile-based groups only to contrast genomic and ecological patterns at the extremes of the continuum.*


**At this stage, we have rigorously defined and validated a continuous genomic trophic index using independent indicator species; subsequent analyses focus on applying this index to community-wide datasets rather than further validating its construction.**

# assess the changes on species level moving from incomplete observed to 100% complete estimated lake taxa
```{r}
# =============================================================================
# BLOCK 8 (COMPACT) — One figure per PCA trait (Lake Zurich): Obs vs Est + shift + regression
#
# You asked for ONE exported figure per trait that enters the PCA:
#   - Estimated_Genome_Size  (proxy for "true" genome size; compare to GENOME_LENGTH)
#   - NUM_SIGMA             (Obs vs Est)
#   - TOTAL_TRNA            (Obs vs Est)
#   - CODING_DENSITY        (no Obs/Est in your table; plotted vs Completeness + distribution)
#
# Output per trait: ONE PDF with 3 panels:
#   A) Observed vs Estimated scatter (or GENOME_LENGTH vs Estimated_Genome_Size)
#   B) Distribution shift (overlay density; simple and clean)
#   C) Change vs Completeness (Delta = Estimated − Observed; regression)
#
# Notes:
#   - For NUM_SIGMA and TOTAL_TRNA we use Obs_* and Est_* columns.
#   - For Estimated_Genome_Size we compare GENOME_LENGTH (observed assembly length)
#     to Estimated_Genome_Size (expected full genome size).
#   - CODING_DENSITY is not "completion-corrected" here (no Est_CODING_DENSITY column),
#     so we show its distribution + relationship to completeness only (no Delta panel).
# =============================================================================

# -----------------------------
# 1) Helper: 3-panel figure for traits with Obs/Est
# -----------------------------
make_tripanel_obs_est <- function(dat, obs_col, est_col, trait_name, out_pdf) {

  dat <- dat %>%
    mutate(Delta = .data[[est_col]] - .data[[obs_col]])

  # A) Obs vs Est
  pA <- ggplot(dat, aes(x = .data[[obs_col]], y = .data[[est_col]])) +
    geom_point(alpha = 0.6) +
    geom_abline(slope = 1, intercept = 0, linetype = "dashed") +
    theme_minimal(base_size = 12) +
    labs(
      title = paste0(trait_name, " — A) Observed vs Estimated"),
      subtitle = "Dashed line = 1:1 (no change)",
      x = "Observed (MAG)",
      y = "Estimated (100% genome)"
    )

  # B) Distribution shift (Observed vs Estimated)
  df_long <- dat %>%
    select(all_of(c(obs_col, est_col))) %>%
    pivot_longer(everything(), names_to = "Version", values_to = "Value") %>%
    mutate(
      Version = ifelse(Version == obs_col, "Observed", "Estimated"),
      Version = factor(Version, levels = c("Observed", "Estimated"))
    )

  pB <- ggplot(df_long, aes(x = Value, color = Version, fill = Version)) +
    geom_density(alpha = 0.25, linewidth = 1) +
    theme_minimal(base_size = 12) +
    theme(legend.position = "top") +
    labs(
      title = paste0(trait_name, " — B) Distribution shift"),
      subtitle = "Overlay densities",
      x = trait_name,
      y = "Density",
      color = NULL,
      fill = NULL
    )

  # C) Delta vs Completeness (regression)
  pC <- ggplot(dat, aes(x = Completeness, y = Delta)) +
    geom_hline(yintercept = 0, linetype = "dashed") +
    geom_point(alpha = 0.6) +
    geom_smooth(method = "lm", se = FALSE) +
    theme_minimal(base_size = 12) +
    labs(
      title = paste0(trait_name, " — C) Correction vs completeness"),
      subtitle = "Delta = Estimated − Observed (linear regression shown)",
      x = "Completeness (%)",
      y = "Delta"
    )

  fig <- (pA | pB) / pC +
    plot_annotation(
      title = paste0("Lake Zurich MAGs: completion correction impact on ", trait_name),
      theme = theme(plot.title = element_text(face = "bold", size = 13))
    )

  ggsave(out_pdf, fig, width = 10.5, height = 7.5, units = "in")
  print(fig)
  invisible(fig)
}

# -----------------------------
# 2) Helper: 3-panel figure for genome size proxy (GENOME_LENGTH vs Estimated_Genome_Size)
# -----------------------------
make_tripanel_genomesize <- function(dat, out_pdf) {

  dat <- dat %>%
    mutate(Delta = Estimated_Genome_Size - GENOME_LENGTH)

  pA <- ggplot(dat, aes(x = GENOME_LENGTH, y = Estimated_Genome_Size)) +
    geom_point(alpha = 0.6) +
    geom_abline(slope = 1, intercept = 0, linetype = "dashed") +
    theme_minimal(base_size = 12) +
    labs(
      title = "Estimated genome size — A) Assembly length vs estimated full size",
      subtitle = "Dashed line = 1:1 (no missing genome implied)",
      x = "Observed assembly length (GENOME_LENGTH, bp)",
      y = "Estimated full genome size (bp)"
    )

  df_long <- dat %>%
    select(GENOME_LENGTH, Estimated_Genome_Size) %>%
    pivot_longer(everything(), names_to = "Version", values_to = "Value") %>%
    mutate(
      Version = recode(Version,
                       GENOME_LENGTH = "Observed (assembly)",
                       Estimated_Genome_Size = "Estimated (full genome)"),
      Version = factor(Version, levels = c("Observed (assembly)", "Estimated (full genome)"))
    )

  pB <- ggplot(df_long, aes(x = Value, color = Version, fill = Version)) +
    geom_density(alpha = 0.25, linewidth = 1) +
    theme_minimal(base_size = 12) +
    theme(legend.position = "top") +
    labs(
      title = "Estimated genome size — B) Distribution shift",
      subtitle = "Overlay densities",
      x = "Genome size (bp)",
      y = "Density",
      color = NULL,
      fill = NULL
    )

  pC <- ggplot(dat, aes(x = Completeness, y = Delta)) +
    geom_hline(yintercept = 0, linetype = "dashed") +
    geom_point(alpha = 0.6) +
    geom_smooth(method = "lm", se = FALSE) +
    theme_minimal(base_size = 12) +
    labs(
      title = "Estimated genome size — C) Missing genome length vs completeness",
      subtitle = "Delta = Estimated_Genome_Size − GENOME_LENGTH",
      x = "Completeness (%)",
      y = "Missing genome length (bp)"
    )

  fig <- (pA | pB) / pC +
    plot_annotation(
      title = "Lake Zurich MAGs: completion correction impact on genome size",
      theme = theme(plot.title = element_text(face = "bold", size = 13))
    )

  ggsave(out_pdf, fig, width = 10.5, height = 7.5, units = "in")
  print(fig)
  invisible(fig)
}

# -----------------------------
# 3) Helper: trait with NO Obs/Est (CODING_DENSITY)
#    -> One figure with 2 panels (distribution + vs completeness)
# -----------------------------
make_coding_density_fig <- function(dat, out_pdf) {

  pA <- ggplot(dat, aes(x = CODING_DENSITY)) +
    geom_histogram(bins = 35, color = "white") +
    theme_minimal(base_size = 12) +
    labs(
      title = "CODING_DENSITY — A) Distribution across Lake Zurich MAGs",
      x = "Coding density (%)",
      y = "Number of MAGs"
    )

  pB <- ggplot(dat, aes(x = Completeness, y = CODING_DENSITY)) +
    geom_point(alpha = 0.6) +
    geom_smooth(method = "lm", se = FALSE) +
    theme_minimal(base_size = 12) +
    labs(
      title = "CODING_DENSITY — B) Relationship to completeness",
      subtitle = "Linear regression shown",
      x = "Completeness (%)",
      y = "Coding density (%)"
    )

  fig <- pA / pB +
    plot_annotation(
      title = "Lake Zurich MAGs: coding density (input trait for PCA)",
      theme = theme(plot.title = element_text(face = "bold", size = 13))
    )

  ggsave(out_pdf, fig, width = 8.5, height = 7.5, units = "in")
  print(fig)
  invisible(fig)
}

# =============================================================================
# 4) Build the four figures you requested (ONE PDF each)
# =============================================================================

# (1) Genome size: GENOME_LENGTH vs Estimated_Genome_Size
make_tripanel_genomesize(
  lake_dat,
  out_pdf = "LakeZurich_GenomeSize_GenomeLength_vs_EstimatedGenomeSize.pdf"
)

# (2) TOTAL_TRNA (Obs vs Est)
make_tripanel_obs_est(
  lake_dat,
  obs_col = "Obs_TOTAL_TRNA",
  est_col = "Est_TOTAL_TRNA",
  trait_name = "TOTAL_TRNA",
  out_pdf = "LakeZurich_TOTAL_TRNA_ObsVsEst_Shift_Regression.pdf"
)

# (3) NUM_SIGMA (Obs vs Est)
make_tripanel_obs_est(
  lake_dat,
  obs_col = "Obs_NUM_SIGMA",
  est_col = "Est_NUM_SIGMA",
  trait_name = "NUM_SIGMA",
  out_pdf = "LakeZurich_NUM_SIGMA_ObsVsEst_Shift_Regression.pdf"
)

# (4) CODING_DENSITY (no corrected version available in lake_dat)
make_coding_density_fig(
  lake_dat,
  out_pdf = "LakeZurich_CODING_DENSITY_Distribution_Regression.pdf"
)

cat("\nWrote 4 PDFs:\n",
    " - LakeZurich_GenomeSize_GenomeLength_vs_EstimatedGenomeSize.pdf\n",
    " - LakeZurich_TOTAL_TRNA_ObsVsEst_Shift_Regression.pdf\n",
    " - LakeZurich_NUM_SIGMA_ObsVsEst_Shift_Regression.pdf\n",
    " - LakeZurich_CODING_DENSITY_Distribution_Regression.pdf\n", sep = "")
# =============================================================================
```

```{r}
# =============================================================================
# BLOCK 9 — Lake Zurich trophic index projection (OBS vs EST) + same plots as before
#
# Clean + reviewer-safe:
#   - Uses training-defined model (center/scale/loadings) from BLOCK 6
#   - Projects Lake Zurich twice:
#       OBS: GENOME_LENGTH, CODING_DENSITY, Obs_TOTAL_TRNA, Obs_NUM_SIGMA
#       EST: Estimated_Genome_Size, CODING_DENSITY, Est_TOTAL_TRNA, Est_NUM_SIGMA
#   - Reproduces your exact plot types, now as 2-panel comparisons (OBS | EST):
#       1) Ranked plot with training anchors
#       2) Training density + Lake density (side-by-side)
#       3) Overlay density (Training vs Lake)
# =============================================================================

suppressPackageStartupMessages({
  library(dplyr)
  library(ggplot2)
  library(ggrepel)
  library(patchwork)
})

# --- training model pieces (from BLOCK 6) ---
center_tr <- trophic_index_model$center
scale_tr  <- trophic_index_model$scale
w_pc1     <- trophic_index_model$pc1_loadings

# --- training anchors (from BLOCK 6 + BLOCK 0) ---
train_scored <- dat_feat_ti %>%
  left_join(train_dat %>% select(Species_ID, Taxonomy), by = "Species_ID") %>%
  mutate(
    Dataset = "Training",
    Label = ifelse(!is.na(Taxonomy) & Taxonomy != "", Taxonomy, Species_ID)
  )

# =============================================================================
# 9.1 Project Lake Zurich twice (OBS and EST), using training centering/scaling
# =============================================================================

# OBS matrix mapped into training variable names
X_obs <- lake_dat %>%
  transmute(
    GENOME_LENGTH  = as.numeric(GENOME_LENGTH),
    CODING_DENSITY = as.numeric(CODING_DENSITY),
    TOTAL_TRNA     = as.numeric(Obs_TOTAL_TRNA),
    NUM_SIGMA      = as.numeric(Obs_NUM_SIGMA)
  ) %>%
  as.matrix()

# EST matrix mapped into training variable names
X_est <- lake_dat %>%
  transmute(
    GENOME_LENGTH  = as.numeric(Estimated_Genome_Size),
    CODING_DENSITY = as.numeric(CODING_DENSITY),
    TOTAL_TRNA     = as.numeric(Est_TOTAL_TRNA),
    NUM_SIGMA      = as.numeric(Est_NUM_SIGMA)
  ) %>%
  as.matrix()

# enforce training order (important)
X_obs <- X_obs[, names(center_tr)]
X_est <- X_est[, names(center_tr)]

# standardize with TRAINING parameters
Z_obs <- sweep(sweep(X_obs, 2, center_tr, "-"), 2, scale_tr, "/")
Z_est <- sweep(sweep(X_est, 2, center_tr, "-"), 2, scale_tr, "/")

# score
lake_scored_obs <- lake_dat %>%
  mutate(TrophicIndex = as.numeric(Z_obs %*% w_pc1),
         Dataset = "LakeZurich_Obs")

lake_scored_est <- lake_dat %>%
  mutate(TrophicIndex = as.numeric(Z_est %*% w_pc1),
         Dataset = "LakeZurich_Est")

# =============================================================================
# 9.2 Plot 1 — Ranked plot with training anchors (OBS | EST)
# =============================================================================

rank_plot <- function(lake_scored, main_title) {

  comb <- bind_rows(
    lake_scored %>% select(Species_ID, TrophicIndex, Dataset),
    train_scored %>% select(Species_ID, TrophicIndex, Dataset, Classification, Label)
  ) %>%
    arrange(TrophicIndex) %>%
    mutate(Rank = row_number())

  ggplot() +
    geom_point(
      data = comb %>% filter(grepl("^LakeZurich", Dataset)),
      aes(Rank, TrophicIndex),
      color = "grey70", alpha = 0.9, size = 2
    ) +
    geom_point(
      data = comb %>% filter(Dataset == "Training"),
      aes(Rank, TrophicIndex, color = Classification),
      size = 3
    ) +
    ggrepel::geom_text_repel(
      data = comb %>% filter(Dataset == "Training"),
      aes(Rank, TrophicIndex, label = Label, color = Classification),
      size = 3,
      max.overlaps = Inf,
      box.padding = 0.4,
      point.padding = 0.2,
      show.legend = FALSE
    ) +
    scale_color_manual(values = trophic_cols) +
    theme_minimal(base_size = 13) +
    labs(
      title = main_title,
      subtitle = "Grey = Lake Zurich MAGs (ranked); Colored + labeled = indicator species (anchors)",
      x = "Rank along combined trophic index (low → high)",
      y = "TrophicIndex (training PC1 score)"
    ) +
    theme(legend.position = "top")
}

p_rank_obs <- rank_plot(lake_scored_obs, "Lake Zurich trophic index (OBSERVED traits) with training anchors")
p_rank_est <- rank_plot(lake_scored_est, "Lake Zurich trophic index (ESTIMATED traits) with training anchors")
print(p_rank_obs | p_rank_est)

# =============================================================================
# 9.3 Plot 2 — Training density + Lake density (side-by-side) (OBS | EST)
# =============================================================================

density_pair <- function(lake_scored, title_txt) {

  p_den_train <- ggplot(train_scored, aes(TrophicIndex)) +
    geom_density(linewidth = 1) +
    theme_minimal(base_size = 13) +
    labs(title = "Training density", x = "TrophicIndex", y = "Density")

  p_den_lake <- ggplot(lake_scored, aes(TrophicIndex)) +
    geom_density(linewidth = 1) +
    theme_minimal(base_size = 13) +
    labs(title = "Lake Zurich density", x = "TrophicIndex", y = "Density")

  (p_den_train | p_den_lake) + plot_annotation(title = title_txt)
}

p_den_pair_obs <- density_pair(lake_scored_obs, "Densities (OBSERVED projection)")
p_den_pair_est <- density_pair(lake_scored_est, "Densities (ESTIMATED projection)")
print(p_den_pair_obs)
print(p_den_pair_est)

# =============================================================================
# 9.4 Plot 3 — Overlay density (Training vs Lake) (OBS | EST)
# =============================================================================

density_overlay <- function(lake_scored, lake_label, title_txt) {
  ggplot() +
    geom_density(data = train_scored, aes(TrophicIndex, color = "Training"), linewidth = 1) +
    geom_density(data = lake_scored,  aes(TrophicIndex, color = lake_label), linewidth = 1) +
    scale_color_manual(values = c("Training" = "black", lake_label = "grey50")) +
    theme_minimal(base_size = 13) +
    labs(title = title_txt, x = "TrophicIndex", y = "Density", color = NULL) +
    theme(legend.position = "top")
}

p_ov_obs <- density_overlay(lake_scored_obs, "LakeZurich_Obs", "Overlay density (Training vs Lake Zurich — OBS)")
p_ov_est <- density_overlay(lake_scored_est, "LakeZurich_Est", "Overlay density (Training vs Lake Zurich — EST)")
print(p_ov_obs | p_ov_est)
# =============================================================================
```

### Projection of the genomic trophic index onto Lake Zurich taxa: reviewer-ready interpretation

**What this analysis does**
- Lake Zurich MAGs were **projected into the trophic index space defined exclusively by the training (indicator) species**.
- The training PCA model (trait centering, scaling, and PC1 loadings) was *not re-fit* using lake data.
- This constitutes a true **out-of-sample projection**, analogous to applying a trained model to new data.

**Key observations**
- Lake Zurich taxa span a **broad range of trophic index values**, largely overlapping but extending beyond the training distribution.
- Indicator species appear at biologically sensible anchor positions:
  - Canonical oligotrophs anchor the low end.
  - Genome-rich copiotrophs anchor the high end.
- Lake taxa populate the continuum between and beyond these anchors without forming artificial clusters.

**Density comparison (training vs. lake)**
- The lake distribution is **shifted toward lower trophic index values**, consistent with oligotrophy-dominated lake conditions.
- The overlap between distributions indicates that lake taxa fall within the same genomic strategy space defined by the training set.
- The extended tails (especially toward lower values) suggest **novel or more extreme oligotrophic strategies** not fully represented among indicator species.

**Why this is biologically and statistically sound**
- No retraining or rescaling using lake data → **no circularity or data leakage**.
- The trophic index remains a **single, fixed, interpretable axis**.
- Training species function as **biological reference points**, not classification rules.
- Differences in distributions reflect **ecological structure**, not methodological artifacts.

**Reviewer-facing conclusion**
> *“Projection of Lake Zurich MAGs onto the training-derived genomic trophic index revealed that lake taxa occupy the same continuous genomic strategy space as the indicator species, while exhibiting a distribution skewed toward oligotrophic strategies. The coherent placement of well-characterized indicator taxa as anchors confirms the validity of out-of-sample projection and supports the use of the trophic index for lake-wide comparative analyses.”*

**Bottom line**
- This is exactly the correct and defensible way to extend the index to environmental data.
- The result strengthens (rather than weakens) the claim that the trophic index captures a **generalizable genomic life-history continuum**.

```{r}
# =============================================================================
# BLOCK 10 — Training-anchored operational groups applied to Lake Zurich
# (OBS lake vs Training) AND (EST lake vs Training)
#
# Assumes you already created:
#   - train_scored     (Dataset == "Training", has TrophicIndex)
#   - lake_scored_obs  (Dataset == "LakeZurich_Obs", has TrophicIndex)
#   - lake_scored_est  (Dataset == "LakeZurich_Est", has TrophicIndex)
#
# Output:
#   - Figure 1: OBS lake vs Training (side-by-side)
#   - Figure 2: EST lake vs Training (side-by-side)
# =============================================================================

suppressPackageStartupMessages({
  library(dplyr)
  library(ggplot2)
  library(patchwork)
})

# -----------------------------
# 10.1 Training-anchored quartile cutoffs (fixed reference thresholds)
# -----------------------------
q1_train <- unname(quantile(train_scored$TrophicIndex, probs = 0.25, na.rm = TRUE))
q3_train <- unname(quantile(train_scored$TrophicIndex, probs = 0.75, na.rm = TRUE))

cat("\n[Train-anchored quartiles]\n")
cat(" Q1 (25%) =", round(q1_train, 4), "\n")
cat(" Q3 (75%) =", round(q3_train, 4), "\n")

assign_extremes_trainanchored <- function(df, q1, q3) {
  df %>%
    mutate(
      ExtremeClass = case_when(
        TrophicIndex <= q1 ~ "Oligo_like",
        TrophicIndex >= q3 ~ "Copio_like",
        TRUE ~ "Intermediate"
      ),
      ExtremeClass = factor(ExtremeClass, levels = c("Oligo_like","Intermediate","Copio_like"))
    )
}

# -----------------------------
# 10.2 Helper for subtitle counts
# -----------------------------
fmt_counts <- function(df) {
  df %>% count(ExtremeClass) %>%
    mutate(txt = paste0(ExtremeClass, "=", n)) %>%
    pull(txt) %>%
    paste(collapse = " | ")
}

# -----------------------------
# 10.3 Colors (consistent with palette)
# -----------------------------
extreme_cols <- c(
  Oligo_like = "#2166AC",
  Intermediate = "grey60",
  Copio_like = "#B2182B"
)

# -----------------------------
# 10.4 Plot helper (density with fixed thresholds)
# -----------------------------
density_ext_plot <- function(df, title_txt, subtitle_txt) {
  ggplot(df, aes(TrophicIndex, fill = ExtremeClass, color = ExtremeClass)) +
    geom_density(alpha = 0.4, linewidth = 1) +
    geom_vline(xintercept = c(q1_train, q3_train), linetype = "dashed", linewidth = 0.8) +
    scale_fill_manual(values = extreme_cols, drop = FALSE) +
    scale_color_manual(values = extreme_cols, drop = FALSE) +
    theme_minimal(base_size = 13) +
    labs(
      title = title_txt,
      subtitle = subtitle_txt,
      x = "TrophicIndex (training PC1 score)",
      y = "Density"
    ) +
    theme(legend.position = "top")
}

# =============================================================================
# FIGURE 1 — OBSERVED Lake vs Training
# =============================================================================
train_ext <- assign_extremes_trainanchored(train_scored, q1_train, q3_train)
lake_obs_ext <- assign_extremes_trainanchored(lake_scored_obs, q1_train, q3_train)

sub_train <- paste0("n: ", fmt_counts(train_ext),
                    "   (Q25=", round(q1_train, 2), ", Q75=", round(q3_train, 2), ")")
sub_lake_obs <- paste0("n: ", fmt_counts(lake_obs_ext),
                       "   (Q25=", round(q1_train, 2), ", Q75=", round(q3_train, 2), ")")

p_lake_obs <- density_ext_plot(lake_obs_ext, "Lake Zurich OBS (training-anchored thresholds)", sub_lake_obs)
p_train    <- density_ext_plot(train_ext,    "Training set (thresholds defined here)",       sub_train)

fig_obs <- p_lake_obs | p_train
print(fig_obs)

ggsave("TrainAnchored_Extremes_OBS_Lake_vs_Training.pdf",
       fig_obs, width = 12, height = 5.5, units = "in")

# =============================================================================
# FIGURE 2 — ESTIMATED Lake vs Training
# =============================================================================
lake_est_ext <- assign_extremes_trainanchored(lake_scored_est, q1_train, q3_train)

sub_lake_est <- paste0("n: ", fmt_counts(lake_est_ext),
                       "   (Q25=", round(q1_train, 2), ", Q75=", round(q3_train, 2), ")")

p_lake_est <- density_ext_plot(lake_est_ext, "Lake Zurich EST (training-anchored thresholds)", sub_lake_est)

fig_est <- p_lake_est | p_train
print(fig_est)

ggsave("TrainAnchored_Extremes_EST_Lake_vs_Training.pdf",
       fig_est, width = 12, height = 5.5, units = "in")
```

### Training-anchored operational grouping of Lake Zurich taxa: interpretation and critical assessment

#### What this analysis does
- We applied **fixed, training-derived quartile thresholds** of the genomic trophic index (Q25 = −1.15; Q75 = 1.33) to Lake Zurich species.
- These thresholds were defined **exclusively from the indicator (training) set** and then projected onto the lake community.
- As a result, lake taxa are classified relative to a **global reference space of genomic strategies**, not relative to the lake distribution itself.

#### Interpretation of the results
- Lake Zurich species occupy a **restricted and left-shifted subset** of the trophic index space defined by the training data.
- The lake distribution is strongly skewed toward low index values, indicating dominance of **oligotrophic genomic strategies**.
- Using training-anchored thresholds:
  - The **oligo-like group dominates** the lake community (≈ 100 taxa),
  - A substantial **intermediate group** remains (≈ 150 taxa),
  - **Copio-like strategies are rare** (≈ 15 taxa).
- In contrast, the training set spans the **full continuum** from oligo-like to copio-like strategies, with balanced representation of extremes.
- This asymmetry demonstrates that Lake Zurich does **not mirror the global strategy space**, but instead occupies a biologically constrained subset of it.

#### Why training-anchored thresholds are important
- Defining quartiles from the lake distribution itself would:
  - Redefine “copiotrophic” strategies relative to an already oligotroph-dominated community,
  - Obscure absolute biological meaning,
  - Prevent comparison across systems.
- Training-anchored thresholds preserve **interpretability and comparability** across datasets and environments.
- The approach explicitly treats these groups as **operational extremes along a continuum**, not as discrete ecological categories.

#### Critical assessment (reviewer-facing)
- Strengths:
  - Thresholds are **label-independent and reproducible**.
  - The method respects the **continuous nature** of trophic strategies.
  - Extremes are defined conservatively, avoiding overclassification.
  - Provides a transparent bridge between continuous indices and group-based analyses.
- Limitations:
  - Quartile cutoffs are inherently **arbitrary**, though defensible as operational definitions.
  - Group membership should not be overinterpreted as strict ecological classes.
- These limitations are mitigated by:
  - Prior permutation-based PCA validation,
  - Robustness tests,
  - Clear presentation of the underlying continuous index.

#### Justification for downstream group-based comparisons
- While the trophic index is treated as a **continuous axis**, quartile-based groups provide a practical framework for:
  - Comparing **genomic potential** (e.g., functional gene repertoires),
  - Assessing **realized proteomic investment** across strategy extremes,
  - Visualizing contrasts using boxplots or similar summaries.
- Importantly, such comparisons are **anchored in a validated continuous framework**, ensuring that statistical contrasts do not imply artificial discretization.

#### Take-home message
- Lake Zurich harbors predominantly oligotrophic and intermediate genomic strategies, with only sparse copiotrophic representation.
- Training-anchored operational groups allow biologically meaningful, reviewer-safe comparisons while fully respecting the continuum of trophic adaptation.
- This framework is well suited for subsequent analyses linking genomic capacity to realized proteomic function.

```{r}
# =============================================================================
# BLOCK 10A — MONEY FIGURE (OBSERVED Lake Zurich projection) + exports
# Reviewer-safe, fully hardcoded, no NSE tricks.
#
# Inputs assumed to exist:
#   train_scored      (Training; has TrophicIndex, Classification optional, Label optional)
#   lake_scored_obs   (Lake OBS; has Species_ID, TrophicIndex, plus numeric predictors)
#   trophic_cols      (optional; colors for Classification in panel C)
#
# Exports:
#   MoneyFigure_OBS.pdf
#   LakeZurich_RetainedSpecies_TrophicIndex_TrophicGroup_OBS.csv
#   LakeZurich_TrophicIndex_PredictorStats_Summary_OBS.csv
# =============================================================================

suppressPackageStartupMessages({
  library(dplyr)
  library(tidyr)
  library(ggplot2)
  library(ggrepel)
  library(patchwork)
  library(readr)
})

# -----------------------------
# 1) Training-anchored cutoffs (defined ONCE from training)
# -----------------------------
q25_train <- unname(quantile(train_scored$TrophicIndex, 0.25, na.rm = TRUE))
q75_train <- unname(quantile(train_scored$TrophicIndex, 0.75, na.rm = TRUE))

cat("\n[OBS] Training-anchored cutoffs:\n")
cat("  Q25 =", round(q25_train, 4), "\n")
cat("  Q75 =", round(q75_train, 4), "\n")

assign_groups <- function(df) {
  df %>%
    mutate(
      TrophicGroup = case_when(
        TrophicIndex <= q25_train ~ "Oligo_like",
        TrophicIndex >= q75_train ~ "Copio_like",
        TRUE                      ~ "Intermediate"
      ),
      TrophicGroup = factor(TrophicGroup, levels = c("Oligo_like","Intermediate","Copio_like"))
    )
}

train_g <- assign_groups(train_scored)
lake_g  <- assign_groups(lake_scored_obs)

cat("\n[OBS] Group sizes (training):\n")
print(train_g %>% count(TrophicGroup))
cat("\n[OBS] Group sizes (lake):\n")
print(lake_g %>% count(TrophicGroup))

# Colors for operational groups
group_cols <- c(
  Oligo_like   = "#2166AC",
  Intermediate = "grey60",
  Copio_like   = "#B2182B"
)

# -----------------------------
# 2) MONEY FIGURE PANELS
# -----------------------------

# Panel A — Density overlay (Training vs Lake OBS)
pA <- ggplot() +
  geom_density(data = train_g, aes(TrophicIndex, color = "Training"), linewidth = 1) +
  geom_density(data = lake_g,  aes(TrophicIndex, color = "LakeZurich_OBS"), linewidth = 1) +
  geom_vline(xintercept = c(q25_train, q75_train), linetype = "dashed", linewidth = 0.8) +
  scale_color_manual(values = c(Training = "black", LakeZurich_OBS = "grey50")) +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 12) +
  labs(
    title = "A) Calibration + occupancy of index space",
    subtitle = "Lake Zurich projection uses OBSERVED genomic traits (dashed = training Q25/Q75)",
    x = "TrophicIndex (training PC1 score)",
    y = "Density",
    color = NULL
  ) +
  theme(legend.position = "top")

# Panel B — Lake OBS density colored by training-anchored groups
fmt_counts <- function(df) {
  tab <- df %>% count(TrophicGroup)
  paste0(tab$TrophicGroup, "=", tab$n, collapse = " | ")
}
sub_lake  <- paste0("n: ", fmt_counts(lake_g),  "   (Q25=", round(q25_train,2), ", Q75=", round(q75_train,2), ")")

pB <- ggplot(lake_g, aes(TrophicIndex, fill = TrophicGroup, color = TrophicGroup)) +
  geom_density(alpha = 0.35, linewidth = 1) +
  geom_vline(xintercept = c(q25_train, q75_train), linetype = "dashed", linewidth = 0.8) +
  scale_fill_manual(values = group_cols, drop = FALSE) +
  scale_color_manual(values = group_cols, drop = FALSE) +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 12) +
  labs(
    title = "B) Lake Zurich (OBS) with training-anchored thresholds",
    subtitle = sub_lake,
    x = "TrophicIndex (training PC1 score)",
    y = "Density"
  ) +
  theme(legend.position = "top")

# Panel C — Ranked axis with training anchors (lake grey; training colored/labeled)
use_class_colors <- ("Classification" %in% names(train_g)) && exists("trophic_cols")
comb <- bind_rows(
  lake_g  %>% transmute(Species_ID, TrophicIndex, Dataset = "LakeZurich_OBS",
                        Classification = NA_character_, Label = NA_character_),
  train_g %>% transmute(
    Species_ID = as.character(Species_ID),
    TrophicIndex,
    Dataset = "Training",
    Classification = if ("Classification" %in% names(train_g)) as.character(Classification) else NA_character_,
    Label = if ("Label" %in% names(train_g)) as.character(Label) else as.character(Species_ID)
  )
) %>% arrange(TrophicIndex) %>% mutate(Rank = row_number())

pC <- ggplot() +
  geom_point(
    data = comb %>% filter(Dataset == "LakeZurich_OBS"),
    aes(Rank, TrophicIndex),
    color = "grey75", alpha = 0.8, size = 1.8
  ) +
  geom_point(
    data = comb %>% filter(Dataset == "Training"),
    aes(Rank, TrophicIndex, color = if (use_class_colors) Classification else Dataset),
    size = 2.8
  ) +
  ggrepel::geom_text_repel(
    data = comb %>% filter(Dataset == "Training" & !is.na(Label)),
    aes(Rank, TrophicIndex, label = Label,
        color = if (use_class_colors) Classification else Dataset),
    size = 3,
    max.overlaps = Inf,
    box.padding = 0.35,
    point.padding = 0.15,
    show.legend = FALSE
  ) +
  geom_hline(yintercept = c(q25_train, q75_train), linetype = "dashed", linewidth = 0.6) +
  { if (use_class_colors) scale_color_manual(values = trophic_cols) else scale_color_manual(values = c(Training = "black")) } +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 12) +
  labs(
    title = "C) Ranked axis with training anchors",
    subtitle = "Grey = Lake Zurich (OBS); labeled points = indicator species (dashed = Q25/Q75)",
    x = "Rank along combined trophic index (low → high)",
    y = "TrophicIndex (training PC1 score)",
    color = NULL
  ) +
  theme(legend.position = "top")

# Panel D — Bootstrap stability of cutoffs + near-boundary lake fraction
set.seed(123)
B <- 2000
boot_cutoffs <- replicate(B, {
  idx <- sample(seq_len(nrow(train_g)), replace = TRUE)
  x <- train_g$TrophicIndex[idx]
  c(q25 = unname(quantile(x, 0.25, na.rm = TRUE)),
    q75 = unname(quantile(x, 0.75, na.rm = TRUE)))
}) %>% t() %>% as.data.frame()

ci_q25 <- quantile(boot_cutoffs$q25, c(0.025, 0.975), na.rm = TRUE)
ci_q75 <- quantile(boot_cutoffs$q75, c(0.025, 0.975), na.rm = TRUE)

eps <- 0.1 * IQR(train_g$TrophicIndex, na.rm = TRUE)
near_boundary <- lake_g %>%
  mutate(near = (abs(TrophicIndex - q25_train) <= eps) | (abs(TrophicIndex - q75_train) <= eps))
near_frac <- mean(near_boundary$near, na.rm = TRUE)
near_n <- sum(near_boundary$near, na.rm = TRUE)

boot_long <- boot_cutoffs %>%
  pivot_longer(cols = everything(), names_to = "Cutoff", values_to = "Value") %>%
  mutate(Cutoff = recode(Cutoff, q25 = "Q25", q75 = "Q75"))

pD <- ggplot(boot_long, aes(Value, fill = Cutoff)) +
  geom_histogram(bins = 35, alpha = 0.55, color = "grey30") +
  geom_vline(xintercept = q25_train, linetype = "dashed", linewidth = 0.8) +
  geom_vline(xintercept = q75_train, linetype = "dashed", linewidth = 0.8) +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 12) +
  labs(
    title = "D) Cutoff stability (bootstrap on training set)",
    subtitle = paste0(
      "OBS projection; 95% CI: Q25[", round(ci_q25[1],2), ", ", round(ci_q25[2],2),
      "], Q75[", round(ci_q75[1],2), ", ", round(ci_q75[2],2),
      "]; Lake near-boundary=", near_n, "/", nrow(lake_g), " (", round(100*near_frac,1), "%), eps=", round(eps,2)
    ),
    x = "Bootstrap cutoff value",
    y = "Count",
    fill = NULL
  ) +
  theme(legend.position = "top")

# Assemble money figure
money_figure_obs <- (pA | pB) / (pC | pD) +
  plot_annotation(
    title = "Training-defined genomic trophic index (Lake Zurich OBS projection)",
    subtitle = "Index + thresholds defined in indicator species; applied unchanged to Lake Zurich taxa"
  )

print(money_figure_obs)

# -----------------------------
# 3) EXPORTS (OBS)
# -----------------------------
ggsave("MoneyFigure_OBS.pdf", money_figure_obs, width = 13, height = 10, units = "in")

lake_retained_tbl_obs <- lake_g %>%
  transmute(
    Species_ID = as.character(Species_ID),
```
